label.title: Spectral Cues for Speech vs Nonspeech Detection
label.summary: This memo discusses why pure energy-based detection fails to distinguish low-energy speech from nonspeech noise, proposing that spectral slope characteristics can help differentiate them. Unvoiced speech sounds (like the start of 'S') tend to have high-frequency/high-pass characteristics, while background noise and rumble tend to be low-frequency/low-pass, and this approach echoes techniques from Rabiner and Schafer's older work.
key_points: Pure energy-based analysis cannot reliably distinguish low-energy speech from nonspeech noise.
Most nonspeech noises exhibit a low-pass (downward sloping) spectral characteristic.
Low-energy unvoiced speech components (e.g., onset of an 'S' sound) tend to have a high-pass (upward sloping) spectral characteristic.
Background rumble and noise are predominantly low-frequency.
Spectral slope alone is insufficient, but combined with energy and timing information it becomes a useful detection method.
This combined approach is similar to techniques described in Rabiner and Schafer's work from roughly 25 years prior.

persona: a coffee-shop owner in a small town
topic: notes from a course, workshop, or tutorial they are taking
setting: right after the event they are talking about
text:
Oh man, I just got back from the Small Business Owners Workshop at the community center and I am so fired up right now. The instructor, Sarah Chen, she's from Portland actually, she showed us this whole thing about social media scheduling and I was like, "Wait, we can do that?" 

She said we should post at least three times a week and I'm like, "Three times?" She's like, "Yeah, and we can do it all at once on Sundays." I'm thinking, "That's a lot of work." But then she showed us this template she made and it was so simple, I was like, "Oh my god, I can do this." 

There's this guy in town, Mike, from the hardware store, he was like, "Well I'm not really into that social media stuff." Sarah's like, "Well then you can just post one photo of your shop every week." I was like, "Yes!" That makes so much sense. She also mentioned something about Facebook groups and I'm like, "What Facebook groups?" 

She said we could create a local business Facebook group for the whole downtown area and I'm like, "Wait, really?" She said we should start with just five or six businesses, but I'm thinking, "We could do ten!" 

She gave us all these printouts with calendar templates and I'm just so excited to try this out. I've been thinking about doing more social media, but I kept putting it off because it seemed so complicated. But this was actually kind of easy. I'm going to try this next week, maybe even today. I already have three posts planned.

persona: a nurse who works night shifts at a regional hospital
topic: money and budget planning
setting: right after the event they are talking about
text:
Okay so I just got back from the budget meeting with Dr. Martinez and the finance team and honestly I'm kind of overwhelmed by how much money we're supposedly saving but also how much we're supposedly spending. The main thing is we're switching to the new electronic health records system next month and the training costs are going to be around twelve thousand dollars for the staff training alone. That's for like twenty-three nurses and eight doctors so that's about five hundred dollars per person which seems kind of high to me but whatever.

Also we're going to be getting rid of the old paper charts which is good because I'm tired of having to track down those things when patients are in the ER but apparently we're not going to be able to keep the old printer that's in the main office. It's a thirty-year-old HP printer that's been dying on us for like six months now but they're saying it's not compatible with the new system and it's going to cost another two thousand dollars to get that upgraded.

Dr. Martinez said we're going to be able to hire one more part-time nurse for the night shift but that's going to cost us about fifty dollars an hour for the extra hour we need to cover. That's not too bad compared to the other expenses but I think we might have to cut back on the supplies budget a bit. They want us to get more generic medications which I don't mind but it's going to be hard to get the right dosage from the pharmacy sometimes because they're not always as flexible with the older formulations.

Anyway I'm going to try to figure out what I can do with the budget we have left. I'm going to go to the hospital supply store tomorrow and see if they have any deals on the blood pressure cuffs that I keep buying because the ones we have are just falling apart.

Hey, just finished my first marathon and I'm absolutely buzzing, you know? I can't believe I actually did it, I mean, it was really hard but I did it. I'm already thinking about my next big adventure though, and I'm planning to go to Costa Rica next month for a week of hiking and some trail running. I'm staying at this little eco-lodge called La Vida Verde, which is supposed to be really beautiful. It's about 700 meters above sea level, so I think it'll be a good challenge. I'm hoping to do some of the longer trails there, maybe like 8-10 kilometers.

I want to try to maintain my training schedule while I'm there, so I'm going to pack my trail running shoes and my Garmin watch. I've been doing about 50 miles a week lately, so I think I'll be able to keep that up. I want to get some good photos too, so I'm bringing my camera and some extra batteries.

I'm really excited about this trip, you know, it's going to be a great way to mix up my training and get some new experiences. I'm already making my list of places to visit and activities to try. It's going to be amazing, I'm really looking forward to it.

Okay, I need to go now and clean up and get some rest. I'm going to be up at 4 AM tomorrow for another long run. I want to be ready for my next challenge. Talk to you later.

text: Okay so I'm still working on this stoichiometry problem from yesterday's lab and I can't figure out why the calculations aren't matching up with the actual results, I think it's because I used the wrong molar mass for the sodium hydroxide, I was looking at the periodic table and I think I grabbed the wrong number and then when I double-checked it was still wrong so I had to go back and recalculate everything from scratch, I was supposed to be done by 7 PM but now it's 11 PM and I'm just getting frustrated because I have three different sections today and I need to figure out how to explain this to them without making them feel like they're doing it wrong, yeah I was trying to show them the limiting reactant concept with the magnesium and hydrochloric acid but one of the groups got a very different result than expected and when I went over to check their setup I saw they had used the wrong volume measurement from the burette, they were reading it from the wrong side, and it's not like they're being careless, I think it's just that they're not used to seeing the meniscus at eye level yet, and it's a tricky measurement, so I had to explain it again, but then I realized that the whole class was struggling with that particular calculation, so I'm going to have to go back and do a quick review tomorrow morning, maybe create some extra practice problems, the thing is, I should have caught this issue sooner, I should have been more careful about double-checking the materials list before the lab started, and honestly I'm just tired now, I can see myself running into the same issue with the next group I'm going to work with tomorrow, so I better write down exactly how to approach it step by step, so I don't have to spend the whole period repeating myself, okay, that's it for now, I think I'll just take a quick break and then continue working on this.
